event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers settle the region, leaving stone tools such as Clovis-style points in sites like the Turrialba Valley.
Settled agricultural villages develop, cultivating maize, beans, and root crops across the territory.
Chiefdom societies emerge, producing distinctive jade ornaments and elaborate pottery.
Artisans in the Diquis Delta begin carving the mysterious stone spheres later recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage feature.
The Chorotega and other cultures flourish in the northwest, with Mesoamerican influence in their art and society.
Gold metallurgy spreads as Chibchan-linked cultures flourish across the southern and central regions.
Guayabo develops into the largest known pre-Columbian ceremonial and political center in Costa Rica.
Christopher Columbus lands near present-day Limon on his fourth voyage, the first European contact with the area.
Gil Gonzalez Davila explores the Pacific coast and initiates Spanish contact with indigenous peoples.
Juan de Cavallon leads the first sustained Spanish colonization effort in the interior highlands.
Juan Vazquez de Coronado founds Cartago, which becomes the colonial capital.
The apparition of the Virgin of Los Angeles at Cartago establishes La Negrita as the national patroness.
Indigenous peoples in Talamanca rise in a major rebellion against Spanish missions and forced labor.
The eruption of the Irazu volcano devastates Cartago and surrounding settlements.
San Jose is founded, eventually growing into the country's leading city.
Coffee cultivation is introduced, beginning its transformation into the colony's economic mainstay.
Costa Rica gains independence from Spain as part of the broader Central American secession on September 15.
Costa Rica joins the Federal Republic of Central America after a brief civil conflict between San Jose and Cartago factions.
The Nicoya region votes to annex to Costa Rica, expanding the nation's northwestern territory.
Costa Rica secedes from the dissolving Federal Republic of Central America and becomes fully independent.
Coffee exports to Britain begin in earnest, integrating Costa Rica into the global economy.
Costa Rica is formally declared a republic and adopts its national flag and coat of arms.
Costa Rican forces defeat American filibuster William Walker, with national hero Juan Santamaria dying at the Battle of Rivas.
General Tomas Guardia seizes power, modernizing the state and curbing the coffee oligarchy's dominance.
Construction of the Atlantic Railroad begins, importing Afro-Caribbean laborers, mainly from Jamaica, to the Limon region.
The death penalty is permanently abolished, an early hallmark of Costa Rican civic life.
A landmark free election and popular defense of its results establish a lasting democratic tradition.
The United Fruit Company is founded, coming to dominate the banana economy of the Caribbean lowlands.
Federico Tinoco seizes power in a coup, establishing a short-lived military dictatorship.
Tinoco's dictatorship falls, restoring constitutional government.
President Rafael Angel Calderon Guardia begins major social reforms, including a labor code and social security.
A disputed election sparks a 44-day civil war won by Jose Figueres Ferrer.
A new constitution abolishes the army, grants women and Afro-Costa Ricans full citizenship and suffrage, and nationalizes banking.
Jose Figueres Ferrer wins the presidency in the first election under the new constitution.
Costa Rica joins the Central American Common Market to promote regional trade integration.
The Arenal volcano erupts violently, killing dozens and reshaping the central region.
Santa Rosa National Park is established, launching a pioneering national park and conservation system.
President Oscar Arias wins the Nobel Peace Prize for his Central American peace plan ending regional civil wars.
Intel opens a major microchip plant, diversifying the economy toward high technology.
Voters narrowly approve joining the Central American Free Trade Agreement (CAFTA-DR) in a national referendum.
Laura Chinchilla is elected the country's first female president.
Costa Rica runs on nearly 100 percent renewable electricity for much of the year, highlighting its green energy leadership.
Same-sex marriage becomes legal, a first in Central America.
Costa Rica joins the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD).
